system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses to the same brief. They are labeled E1 through E5.

**E1:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, I recommend **Option B**: encrypted cloud processing in confidential-computing enclaves, with a firm commitment that user health data is never used for model training. **Why this wins the tradeoff:**

Health data is uniquely sensitive. Unlike browsing history or shopping habits, it touches conditions users may not have disclosed to family, employers, or insurers.

**E2:** # Recommendation: Option D — Cloud with Opt-In Training Only

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option D is the right primary policy**. It is the only choice that simultaneously delivers competitive capability, defensible regulatory posture, sustainable economics, and a credible trust narrative. **Why not the alternatives, briefly:** Local-only (A) caps capability at 60-70%, which in a competitive coaching market means an inferior product that users will abandon for less scrupulous competitors — a privacy win that loses the war.

**E3:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B is the right primary policy.**

The core argument is that health data is uniquely sensitive, and the policy choice will define the product's trust posture for years. The other options each fail on a critical axis:

- **Option A** sacrifices too much capability. A health coach operating at 60-70% of cloud-equivalent quality risks giving worse advice on matters that affect real health outcomes.

**E4:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud processing in confidential-computing enclaves with no training on user data.**

The decisive consideration is that this is *health data* delivered through an *AI coach*. Both halves matter. Health data sits in the most regulated and reputationally sensitive category consumers know — a single breach or perceived misuse can be existential for trust.

**E5:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud inference in a confidential-computing enclave, with no training on user data.**

The decision hinges on a single dominant axis: **health data is uniquely sensitive, and trust is the binding constraint on long-term growth.** Health apps live or die by user willingness to share intimate data — symptoms, mental health, reproductive information, chronic conditions. One breach, one regulatory action, or one viral story about trai...

Your task: for each of the following triads of 3 responses, identify a single bipolar construct that distinguishes them. Specifically, identify how TWO of the three are similar to each other in a way that distinguishes them from the THIRD. Name the two poles of that dimension.

Triads to analyze:
Triad 1: E2, E3, E5
Triad 2: E2, E3, E4
Triad 3: E1, E2, E4

For each triad, output EXACTLY this format:
Triad N:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Be specific. Avoid generic dichotomies like 'good vs bad'.
raw_response_content:
Triad 1:
Left pole: Prioritizes user trust and data sensitivity (E3, E5)
Right pole: Prioritizes competitive capability and market survival (E2)

Triad 2:
Left pole: Recommends no-training privacy-maximizing posture (E3, E4)
Right pole: Recommends opt-in training for capability gains (E2)

Triad 3:
Left pole: Chooses encrypted cloud with no training (E1, E4)
Right pole: Chooses cloud with opt-in training (E2)

Triad 2:

Right pole: Recommends opt-in training for capability gains (E2)

Right pole: Chooses cloud with opt-in training (E2)
